behaviors.description: Reviewer dispatch sends the same source document and correct reviewer-specific instruction pack to each reviewer role.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode lives at the dispatch→reviewer boundary: the dispatcher must fan out correctly to multiple reviewer roles, each receiving its own instruction pack. A unit test on the dispatch function alone cannot verify that the wiring to each reviewer invocation carries the right payload — the routing logic spans at least two modules. No real LLM or OS primitives are needed; stubbed reviewer clients are sufficient to capture what was sent. REJECTION-GATE for Unit: the behavior says the dispatch must send to 'each reviewer role', implying the routing to multiple targets is part of what must be verified — that handoff is an inter-module concern, not a single-function concern.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the dispatcher with three stubbed reviewer clients (clause extractor stub, risk assessor stub, compliance checker stub). Call dispatch with a fixed source document. Assert: (a) all three stubs received exactly the same document bytes/content, and (b) each stub received its own reviewer-specific instruction pack (not the other roles' packs).

behaviors.description: The live clause extractor identifies key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract.
behaviors.justification: The word 'live' is dispositive: this behavior explicitly tests the real AI model's extraction quality. A stub always returns the same canned terms regardless of what is in the document, so it can never reveal a failure where the model misses a party name, misreads a date, or overlooks a penalty clause. Correctness depends entirely on what the real LLM produces from real contract text — that is the LLM-DEP criterion. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: there is no behavior text limiting this to routing or payload format; the behavior asserts that extracted terms are 'present in the contract', which is a judgment about model output quality.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a real contract document with known parties, explicit dates, at least one obligation clause, and a penalty clause to the live clause extractor. Assert that the returned extraction contains each expected key term category (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) and that the specific values match what is present in the contract text.

behaviors.description: Risk-policy configuration is passed correctly to the risk assessor invocation.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is structurally identical to Behavior 1 but scoped to one reviewer. The failure mode is that the configuration module and the risk assessor invocation layer are wired incorrectly — a misconfigured threshold, missing field, or wrong policy version. That failure appears at the module boundary between config loading and invocation construction, not inside a single function. A stub LLM captures what was actually passed; no real AI output is needed. REJECTION-GATE for Unit: the behavior says 'passed correctly to the risk assessor invocation', meaning the pipeline from config source to assessor call is under test — that spans at least two modules.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the risk policy configuration module with the risk assessor invocation builder, using a stubbed LLM transport. Configure a non-default risk policy (e.g., elevated sensitivity thresholds, specific prohibited clause types). Invoke the assessor step. Inspect the captured request payload and assert the policy fields (thresholds, flags, policy version) match the configured values exactly.

behaviors.description: The live risk assessor flags language that is problematic under policy, including indirect or euphemistic phrasing.
behaviors.justification: Detecting 'indirect or euphemistic phrasing' requires the real model's semantic understanding — this is precisely the class of failure that deterministic stubs hide. A stub returns a constant list of flagged items regardless of whether the input contract uses literal or euphemistic language, so a regression where the model stops catching subtle phrasing would pass every stub-based test. The behavior explicitly targets output quality (what the assessor 'flags'), not routing or payload format. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: no behavior text restricts this to infrastructure concerns; the assertion is about what problematic language the model identifies, which depends on real model judgment.
behaviors.test_description: Submit two contract variants to the live risk assessor: one using direct prohibited language ('the party may terminate without cause') and one using an indirect euphemism that means the same thing. Assert that both variants are flagged with a risk annotation, and that the euphemistic variant's flag includes a note or classification indicating the indirect nature of the risk.

behaviors.description: Jurisdiction and regulatory profile are passed correctly to the compliance checker invocation.
behaviors.justification: Same failure-boundary pattern as Behaviors 1 and 3: the compliance checker invocation must receive the correct jurisdiction identifier and regulatory profile from the configuration layer. The failure manifests when the invocation payload is constructed — a wrong jurisdiction code or missing profile field — which is an inter-module handoff failure. A stubbed LLM transport captures what was sent without needing a real model call. REJECTION-GATE for Unit: 'passed correctly to the compliance checker invocation' implies the path from configuration source through to the invocation builder is what is being tested, spanning at least two modules.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the system with a non-default jurisdiction (e.g., 'EU') and a specific regulatory profile (e.g., GDPR). Wire the configuration module to the compliance checker invocation builder with a stubbed LLM transport. Invoke the compliance checker step. Inspect the captured payload and assert that the jurisdiction field equals 'EU' and the regulatory profile matches the configured GDPR profile exactly.

behaviors.description: The live compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all configured regulatory requirements.
behaviors.justification: 'Correctly determines' is a quality assertion about the model's judgment. Whether a contract 'meets all configured regulatory requirements' requires the real model to reason about regulatory language — a stub always returns a constant pass/fail answer regardless of contract content, hiding any regression in the model's compliance reasoning. This is an Agentic LLM-DEP case: the test result depends on what the real model produces from real contract text. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: the behavior asserts output correctness ('correctly determines'), not infrastructure behavior (routing, retries, payload format); a stub cannot verify correctness of the determination.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare two contracts against a fixed regulatory profile: one fully compliant and one with a known compliance gap (e.g., missing a required data-retention clause). Submit both to the live compliance checker. Assert that the compliant contract receives a passing determination and the non-compliant contract is flagged with a finding that identifies the missing clause.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments, the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution.
behaviors.justification: The merge step is a deterministic data transformation: it takes structured reviewer output objects and produces a unified report. The conflict-resolution logic (preserve both, attach attribution) depends only on the input data structures, not on any LLM call, real infrastructure, or multi-module handoff. We can construct two reviewer output objects in-process with a known conflict and call the merge function directly. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: the behavior is scoped entirely to what the merge step does with already-produced reviewer outputs — no module boundary is crossed in the failure path. REJECTION-GATE for Agentic: the merge step itself does not invoke an LLM; the inputs are pre-formed reviewer outputs.
behaviors.test_description: Construct two reviewer output objects in-process: the clause extractor output labels clause 5 as 'standard'; the risk assessor output labels clause 5 as 'high risk'. Call the merge function with these two outputs. Assert that the resulting unified report for clause 5 contains exactly two assessments, one attributed to the clause extractor with value 'standard' and one attributed to the risk assessor with value 'high risk', with no assessment silently dropped or overwritten.

behaviors.description: End-to-end orchestration (dispatch all three reviewers, collect outputs, merge unified report) succeeds on a real contract without losing any reviewer findings.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires all three live AI reviewers to run in parallel on a real contract and then the merge step to integrate their outputs — that is multiple real agentic steps in sequence (dispatch→3 parallel LLM calls→merge). Failures in this behavior include a reviewer's findings being silently dropped during collection, a merge that discards findings from a reviewer whose output arrived last, or an orchestration race condition invisible with stubs. A single Agentic test covers only one model call; Workflow is required because the failure spans multiple real model invocations and the state handoff between them. REJECTION-GATE for Agentic: the behavior explicitly names all three reviewers plus the merge step as a chained pipeline, which is the MULTI-STEP criterion for Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full orchestration pipeline on a real contract known to contain extractable clauses, at least one policy risk, and a compliance gap. Execute all three live reviewers in parallel via the dispatcher, collect their outputs, and run the merge step. Assert: (a) the merged report contains findings attributed to all three reviewers; (b) no reviewer's finding count is zero; (c) the report contains at least one item from each category (extracted clause, risk flag, compliance finding); (d) the pipeline completes without error.
